topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The ballroom air was thick with the scent of lilies and expensive perfume. I was deep in conversation with Sarah, laughing at some anecdote she was telling, when a shadow fell across my peripheral vision. My breath hitched, a sudden, sharp intake of air that seemed to steal all the oxygen from the room. I slowly turned my head, my smile freezing on my face as my eyes locked onto him. It was him. Mark. He was standing near the entrance, talking to someone, and for a moment, the music seemed to fade into a distant hum. My hand, mid-gesture, stilled completely. I felt a peculiar tightening in my chest, a sudden, unwelcome jolt that made my palms instantly damp. I managed a tight, brittle nod toward him, my mind scrambling to reassemble the social script that had just utterly dissolved.

[story 2]

I was trying to navigate the crowded reception, feeling pleasantly buoyant after a long week, when I bumped squarely into someone. The collision was minor, but it was enough to throw my balance off. I looked up, expecting a waiter or perhaps another guest, but the world seemed to tilt slightly. Standing before me, looking impeccably dressed and utterly familiar, was Alex. His eyes met mine across the expanse of polished wood flooring, and for a beat, time seemed to stretch and warp. A strange, hollow feeling washed over me, making my usual easy demeanor vanish. I mumbled a quick, disjointed apology, my voice sounding strangely thin even to my own ears, and quickly looked away, focusing intently on a distant floral arrangement as if it held the key to immediate escape.

[story 3]

The noise level at the wedding was intense—a vibrant tapestry of chatter, clinking glasses, and upbeat music. I was catching up with a mutual acquaintance near the buffet table, feeling relaxed and content in my own bubble. Then, a familiar silhouette moved into my line of sight. My fork paused halfway to my mouth. My focus snapped entirely to him. There was a distinct hesitation in my movement, a momentary paralysis that made me stand unnaturally still. I felt a flush creep up my neck, and I instinctively took a small step backward, my gaze darting everywhere but toward him, my internal monologue suddenly racing at an alarming, chaotic speed. I forced a small, polite smile, a practiced mask sliding into place, hoping the sheer force of my composure could hide the sudden, unwelcome disorientation taking hold.
